ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general sea bass food product entry; no specific entry for branzino alla mediterranea with caponata and olives. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers sea bass as a species; no composite dish code for branzino alla mediterranea exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Italian seafood dish preparation.

main_ingredients: Mediterranean vegetables (caponata: eggplant, tomato, celery, onion, capers)

serving.description: 1 plate (approximately 350 g including fish fillet and accompaniments)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of branzino alla mediterranea consists of approximately 350 g, comprising a grilled sea bass fillet (approximately 200 g) with a portion of Mediterranean vegetable caponata, Gaeta olives, and capsicum sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, sea bass, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (USDA SR Legacy) combined with USDA FoodData Central entries for caponata vegetables and olive oil components; composite dish estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy and Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for branzino alla mediterranea exists in any approved primary source. Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for grilled sea bass (FDC ID 175159) as the primary component (~57% of dish by weight), combined with USDA entries for eggplant caponata vegetables, Gaeta-style olives, and capsicum/bell pepper sauce components. Olive oil contribution included. Values represent a weighted composite per 100 g of the complete dish. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on grilled fish with low-GI vegetable components
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for branzino alla mediterranea. GI estimated at 30 based on the dominant components: grilled sea bass (protein-dominant, negligible glycemic contribution), Mediterranean vegetable caponata (eggplant GI ~15, tomato GI ~15, capsicum GI ~15), and Gaeta olives (fat-dominant, negligible GI). The overall dish is protein- and fat-dominant with low carbohydrate content (~4.2 g/100 g), yielding a low GI estimate consistent with analogous grilled fish and vegetable dishes in Atkinson et al. 2021. GL per serving = (30 × 14.7 g carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 = 4.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from olives, capers, and capsicum sauce
hypertension.guidance: A 350 g serving provides approximately 1085 mg sodium, primarily from Gaeta olives and capers in the caponata; individuals with hypertension should be aware that this dish contributes a substantial portion of the 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target and should request reduced-salt preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low GI of approximately 30 and only ~14.7 g of carbohydrate per serving, branzino alla mediterranea is a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, as the high protein and healthy monounsaturated fat content from olive oil support glycemic stability and satiety.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids from sea bass and monounsaturated fats from olive oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Branzino (European sea bass) provides omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and the dish is prepared with olive oil rich in monounsaturated fats, both of which are cardioprotective; however, the moderate sodium content from olives and capers warrants attention for individuals with hypertension-related cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Branzino alla Mediterranea is a classic Italian seafood dish featuring a whole or filleted European sea bass (Dicentrarchus labrax, known in Italian as branzino or spigola) that is grilled over high heat to achieve a lightly charred, crispy skin with moist, flaky white flesh. The fish is served alongside a Mediterranean vegetable caponata — a Sicilian-origin sweet-and-sour relish made from eggplant, tomatoes, celery, onion, capers, and olives cooked in olive oil with a touch of vinegar and sugar — accompanied by Gaeta olives, a small, dark, brine-cured Italian olive with a mild, slightly fruity flavour, and a vibrant capsicum (bell pepper) sauce. This version is certified gluten-free (GF), making it suitable for individuals with coeliac disease or gluten sensitivity. The dish is a hallmark of Mediterranean cuisine, celebrated for its nutritional profile: high-quality lean protein from the sea bass, heart-healthy monounsaturated fats from olive oil, and a rich array of antioxidants and dietary fibre from the vegetable components. Clinically, the dish is low in glycemic index (~30), low in saturated fat (~0.9 g/100 g), and provides meaningful omega-3 fatty acids and vitamin B12. The primary nutritional concern is sodium, contributed by the Gaeta olives and capers, which may be relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. In Singapore, this dish is served at Ricciotti - The Riverwalk, an Italian restaurant, and is priced at approximately SGD 37.06.
common_user_queries: Is branzino alla mediterranea low carb?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for branzino alla mediterranea exists in any approved primary nutritional database. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for grilled sea bass as the dominant component, combined with USDA entries for the vegetable and olive components. GI is an analogous estimate based on the dish's macronutrient profile and component GI values from Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium rather than low because the primary ingredient (grilled sea bass) has strong USDA coverage and the vegetable components are well-characterised.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input value 'Grains and staples' to 'Fish and seafood', which accurately reflects the primary ingredient (European sea bass). 2. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as grilled sea bass and olive oil preparations contain negligible trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data. 3. All -1 sentinel values reflect nutrients not individually reported in the composite estimation basis: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, B-vitamins, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and are provided as best-effort values rather than -1, given that USDA SR Legacy does report these for grilled sea bass and the vegetable components. 4. Sodium is moderate-to-high due to Gaeta olives and capers; individuals on sodium-restricted diets should note this. 5. The dish is confirmed gluten-free as described by the restaurant. 6. GI set to 30 as an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GL per serving calculated as (30 × 14.7) / 100 = 4.4 g. 7. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03302051 is used as closest parent for sea bass food product; no composite dish code exists. FoodEx2 code A00LC covers sea bass species-level classification.
